result:
The whistle of the 6:15 a.m. kettle always arrives before the sun does. In these thirty square metres a few floors above the tram line, morning is not just a time but a kind of weather that settles in the lungs. I open the balcony door to let it in—the smell of warm asphalt cooling after a humid night, wet newsprint steaming where the delivery cyclist folded it into a mailbox. Everything is marginally soft, like watercolour edges. For years I thought this softness was a weakness about the city itself, a collective giving-in to heat and humidity; I have since decided it is only patience. The city waits—not as we wait in queues, shifting anxiously from foot to foot, but as a cat waits, eyes half-closed, attuned to the slightest compact vibration of birdwing in dust-flecked sun.

There is, apparently, a limit to the number of times a human heart can break before it grows gilt along the fissures—shatter lines becoming seams of gold, the Japanese saying goes. I have counted mine carefully: once for every friendship that drifted under the colossal silence of careers and continents; once for every impossible attachment to someone who kept their life lightly compartmentalised, like a suitcase that could be zipped or unzipped in minutes. And yet every dawn the organ knocks at its cage, unruly, refusing even the dignity of ceramic irreparableness. I brew coffee in the single chipped press, watch the grounds bloom outwards like planets asserting gravitational dominance. A string of small galaxies inside a glass beaker. Maybe cosmology starts exactly here, in kitchenettes too cramped for anything except boiling water. First there is nothing, then there is a dark bloom expanding, then cream-coloured swirls form spiral arms around the void.

I leave the apartment earlier than necessary. A quarantine in the city left me irritably aware of stored air; I still push through doors as though expecting escape velocities. Down four flights sagging with monsoon moisture and the smoky afterthought of someone’s 2 a.m. curry, I emerge onto the footpath. The first blow of sunlight is full-contact; I recoil reflexively before walking straight into itsören-sweet smog. A builder, pale dust on his brow like mismatched foundation, nods because we recognise one another only in the way strangers recognise the recurring actors in each other’s daily mumblecore. I pass the corner cart that sells coconut-dipped fritters. The owner, Devi, hands the first one of each batch to an old stray the colour of thunderclouds who waits with noble patience—I once saw the man wear an entire plate on his head rather than let the dog go hungry. I nod at both of them. Neither bows. We are commoners in the same kingdom of briefly merciful shadows.  

At the river it is always seven degrees cooler, as though climate is negotiable when water is willing to mediate. I sit on the concrete bench where the algae creeps up the banks like inexperienced graffiti, green across pale grey. A boy sells jasmine bracelets from a wicker basket; I buy one, slide it over my wrist, and feel instantly ridiculous but almost ceremonially enrolled in the day. Sometimes, rarely, I write on napkins. Mostly I watch dragons curve between skyscrapers: construction cranes performing slow calligraphy against low cloud. My sister once said cities were invented for people who’d misunderstood smallness, whose nightmares expanded with too much rural sky. Maybe so. I feel safest when surrounded by millions who have no particular interest in me, just as benthic creatures drift unremarked in black water, bioluminescent only when they choose to be.

The café where I do dayshift proofreading has plywood salvaged from demolished churches for its interior tables—the grain of a hundred former confessional booths lives on inside gossip about rent hikes and casting calls. Milo, the owner, believes in fermentation the way others believe in portions of scripture: kombucha apostles in glass jars haloed by LED grow-lights; kimchi breathing quietly in the cool room like penitents fasting. We take our queues from orphaned cultures of bacteria claiming real estate. I like the symmetry. Late capitalism liquefies us and repurposes us into fizzy drink; meanwhile bacteria colonise our leftovers and produce something tangy and bright. Who’s exploiting whom, I ask you.

At 11:07 an email arrives from a translator in Quebec. She has come to an impasse with the author’s neologism “désertement,” a word coined for both abandonment and bar, simultaneously. French does this thing where verbs become nouns while staying verbs, refusing the fixed floor we erect between action and object. I type back that maybe English solved this elastic longing by inventing the present continuous, as if to concede the admission that nothing is ever quite done. I apologise for the extra syllables. The continuation of departure. Translation, I decide again, is not the ferrying of meaning across a river; it is the negotiation to build a third river where two once stood, complete with reeds and mosquitoes already indigenous to neither side.

By 12:30 the nausea of too much screen sets in. I walk to the art-supply store that smells like an impossible library of trees—cedar shavings, pine gum, ancient bark of extinct forests mashed into paper pulp. I buy nothing, only run my thumb along the sliced spiral sketchbooks whose cardboard backs still soak up the ambient guitar music leaking from upstairs. Somewhere above, a child learns chords that sound like minor-key lullabies written for borrowed sorrow. I remember learning to lie sideways across my twin bed at fourteen, ear to the wall, vibrating with every feet-sore roommate in next year’s unknown dormitory. Hope used to sound like distortion. Maybe still does.  

Across the street is the derelict cinema whose marquee dismantled itself into corrosion and pigeony vowels. Once, the carpets were midnight blue and the seats velvet like overripe plums. These days it hosts midnight pop-up raves where engineers project galaxies against the ruined ceiling and couples make out beneath imagined constellations. Entrance is donated canned goods; exit is remembering hunger can be both literal and figurative. I pass by the chained doors every week and still cannot decide whether what happens inside is vandalism or resurrection. Aren’t most of our living rooms just rebranded churches anyway—flatscreens for stained glass, binge serials for liturgies? We kneel on couches instead of prie-dieux. The popcorn tastes of salt and transubstantiation.

Time dilates when you live six stories up but refuse curtains. The sun becomes a chaperone, then an wallpaper. One year I papered one wall in maps, another in Polaroids taken before smartphones were gods. Different methods of making familiar territory. Somewhere I clipped the tail of a Metro ticket from Berlin dated 8.11.2010; the ink has cracked into tributaries. I remember stepping onto that platform with no luggage other than a battered Lonely Planet whose spine had surrendered at Prague, and we shared the car with three teenagers spraying cologne from miniature aerosol canisters, punctuating the new morning with scent like punctuation marks. First comma after home country; second semicolon before illegal bar. The boys sang poorly a pop song whose lyrics consisted mostly of heart repeated sixteen times, stretched until vowels sounded like skywriting. Somewhere between two stations they rearranged themselves into sleep. When I looked again, the cologne boy had curled against the window, breath fogging glass planted with tiny twin palm prints. Longing fossilised inside condensation.  

Back home in this equatorial tower block I note once more how the doorknob squeaks in E-flat, the exact key of the train lullaby. Sound memory carried halfway around the globe just to find a yellow kitchen door. I stand there, keys in hand, still tasting Berlin’s air blown across eight time zones like balloon mail. Sometimes geography is merely a insistence on resonance. I do not cry about it; the body already hoards humidity like coin. Instead I put water to boil for pasta, add salt until the pot tastes like beach break at low tide. An exhaust fan sighs like a mother who’s finally seated after days.  

At night the wet markets persuade the street into neon haze. Banners hung at diagonals promising 3-D pork and lottery T-shirts. You can buy twenty-five kinds of rice from unmilled ruby grains the colour of hacked pomegranate seeds, to translucent basmati that smells faintly of pandan. Stallholders perform card tricks with plastic bags and receipts. I buy a stalk of lotus root to carve into stamps. There is no practical reason; merely the desire to press a cross-sectioned canal network into saffron ink and see blossoms appear on scrap paper, like cities with radial arteries nobody asked for but now cannot live without.

I own four identical white shirts because decision fatigue accumulates interest; also because the blankness invites projection. When I cycle across the bridge at dusk, headlamp flickering over rivets the colour of dried blood, I imagine the shirts are pages. At red lights, exhaust slides across the cotton until it carries glyphs in grime. I arrive home smelling of motor spirit and carbonised sugar: evidence I have been read, annotated, margin-scribbled by the city’s hurrying hand.  

One shirt still bears the faint bleached mark of a campaign slogan from 2017. I marched that March with two friends who since moved away. We linked arms under banyans older than most constellations. Later the same night at a 24-hour dim sum diner, we invented a religion based on the steam rising from baskets of har gow—the translucent crescents a sacrament, shrimp bright orange prayer beads. Practitioners held silence for exactly four minutes between replenishment cycles. It felt hilariously essential until the police outside dispersed another protest somewhere else; then the laughter tasted like cardboard. Still, somebody tore a hole in the rhetoric of inevitability, stitch by stitch, even if only by demanding bathrooms for trans elders in that fluorescent corridor. The mark faded to peroxide ghost. Mostly I forget, but when I fold laundry the phrase threads itself across fabric like latent Braille.  

Memory sometimes stacks like mise-en-abyme: I remember being six on my grandmother’s veranda. In those years, monsoons were still heroic events thundering across the bay like Wagnerian cavalry. I stood barefoot on cool tiles, watching water lash coconut palms. Nani told me storms were the sky giving itself a haircut; that day it chose mohawk. Electricity went out for forty-eight hours. We ate dinner by lamp flame, shadows capering across vaulted ceilings painted sky-blue decades earlier, now gone tobacco with leaks. Between us and the lantern flame hovered moths fat as hail. Nani recited couplets in a dialect that no longer has a script; I mimic-knotted them around my tongue decades later when speaking to friends, not knowing I carried bruised phonemes in my mouth still colouring every accent I acquired. That night, after rain slackened into caesura, she poured kerosene over termite-ridden beams to keep them from collapsing. The smell combined petrichor and flammable warning. Scent memory so precise I bottled it for years inside a note that simply said summer smells like survival.  

Back to present tense dusk. The shirt is hung up. I cook spinach with garlic that snaps like cheap fireworks. My phone buzzes: group chat debating whether nostalgia is a bourgeois indulgence. One friend currently teaching refugee kids from a makeshift classroom in a disused shopping mall responds with a photo of children re-enacting tag using shadows from a single floodlight. No colour except silver skin and darkness. Caption: “We talk of nostalgia because we still believe tomorrow is a place.” The thread goes quiet until a thumbs-up emoji ambles in like an apology. I set tomatoes to blister on the pan. I do not caption the steam of my kitchen; it is enough I am witness.

At 10 p.m. the upstairs neighbour practices violin again, same Bach prelude turned minor by relentless humidity. At university she studied astrophysics; music is now her rebellion against measurable distances. Sometimes she sways forward and the bow<|reserved_token_163766|>es against climate, then retreats. Through the plaster ceiling I imagine her brain dense as a star catalogue. Sound leaks like constellations half occluded. I write longhand letters I never send because email destroys correlation between scrawl and pulse. By midnight the wrist cramps alphabet railcars running off track, but I persist until ink resembles blood on wood pulp. I address them to every self Iitively past stranger I failed to thank, including the cologne boys in Berlin, the museum guard who looked away when I cried in front of Käthe Kollwitz’s mother embracing dead son, the cat in Kyoto who led me through alleys one evening to avoid a salaryman who had followed since Hakani-cho tram stop. I apologise in swerves of pen for the brevity of kindness and promise I still practice swimming through grief’s narrow lane. I fold pages into lotus boats, float them in a salad bowl of tap water. They disintegrate slowly, fibres drifting like planktonic paper lanterns en route to some fictional horizon.  

Three times a year I take night bus to the coast where bio-luminescent plankton mimic chopped galaxies in the shallows. Amateurs bring buckets expecting fireworks; locals understand the light is shy. You have to apologize for existing in sneakers, stand ankle-deep and wait for pupils to dilate. Then the sand appears star-flecked, and if you walk each footfall bursts turquoise, an alphabet spelling nothing every step. Once a man next to me wept—not dramatic, just tear-tracks gleaming—then confided softly his daughter loved stars but died before second birthday. We never exchanged names; the bioluminescence profiled his grief like silver. At dawn he’d disappeared, but the beach remembered his outline, a depression shaped like the constellation Aquila. I still send spoken postcards into that symmetry whenever sea breeze reaches through my bedroom window via turbine engines of distant freighters.

Online spaces teach me that we are snow globes continuously falling on ourselves. Algorithms plastic-wrap the past into scrollable highlights, severing cause from consequence so that every outrage feels immediate but never cumulative. One morning a thread about gender-neutral housing policies derails into someone claiming architecture is inherently misogynistic because doors impose masculine vertical hierarchies. Within three minutes an elder chimes in with a photo of ceremonial Tajik pillars carved open like lotus ribs to permit even moonlight horizontal passage. Beneath it a comment in Comic Sans simply says “windows are thresholds in drag.” 6k likes. The absurdity does not satiate; it merely displaces. I close the tab and stare out at a construction pit across the street. Men in neon vests are pouring concrete. One wipes his forehead with a flag the exact lime of tropical algae. The metaphor leaks: what bruises beneath ideology is still the wanting muscle. Wipe. Pour. Set.

Some nights I wake to the refrigerator humming in key with the violin upstairs and think the city is harmonising itself insomnia by insomnia. Between 3 and 4 a.m., adrenaline thins into something spiritual but not yet merciful. I sit on the balcony dangling bare feet above traffic long gone. Neon signs ghost on shuttered façades, constellations of commerce blinking still. A solitary pedestrian passes below in headphones, moving arms subtly as though conducting an unseen orchestra. I imagine him hearing the same syncopation I hear: drone of air-con, pulse in throat, the slight buzz from streetlight capacitor. For moments the script switches and the city scores us. Maybe compositions finish only by abandoning bar lines; maybe living is generous improvisation obligating no coda.

The calendar forgives nobody. One July the monsoon fails and water rations begin. We queue with mismatched containers at tankers manned by men who look like sons of former farmers. Their eyes carry dunes. Elderly women hand around peanut candy because the shiver of blood sugar feels like solidarity convertible to moisture. Children play hopscotch beside oil drums labelled potable. A woman in batik sarong recites headlines aloud like misquoted lullabies about glaciers whose elderly memory hovers above their former mass like steam above cooling tectonics. We nod, balancing jugs on hips. My neighbour, an architect, tells me every building blueprint contains a ghost of the river it displaced; we are drinking ancestral runoff todayly disguised. That day the government flies in bottled water branded as charity by conglomerates who lobbied the drought. We take it gratefully, shamefully. Desperation outsources ethics along with manufacturing. I stack the 1.5-litre bottles against my kitchen wall like prayer candles in reverse: we light them by uncapping mouths.  

Two metres away in a different universe, a millionaire live-streams his eco-villa reclaimed from plastic waste. Hashtag #OceanToHome appears on billboards while hand-pumps in peri-urban districts cough rust. I scroll past sunset filtering through an advertisement for carbon offsets, fluorescent orange like sunburn on algorithm. Disconnect. I press the blistered tomatoes into sauce, feed myself with what privilege reduces to mundane verbs: chop, simmer, salt, breathe.

Letters keep coming from the translator, who has now invented the neologism into “unmooreding,” a gerund staying forever in motion yet grammatically homeless. I reply with a photograph of the salad bowl harbouring disintegrated confessions—fibres still swirl like plankton. I type: “I think pdfs can mourn, too.” She answers with the laughing-crying emoji and a video of her son hammering piano keys with both fists, composition entitled “The House That Ran Away.” None of us runs; we merely surrender different organs.  

Autumn here does not arrive with gold leaf, but with a gradation of mildew. One day the air becomes precise, smelling of pencil shavings and distance. I take jacket from suitcase that still smells of cedar mothballs my mother packed in the year I first left for college. It resolutely refuses to fit, but I wear it hanging open like a negotiator with the breeze. The river drifts slower, as though processing metaphors. Dragon boat racers stow paddles; empty buoys gleam like orthodontic overbites. In market, vendors begin arranging pomelos the size of childhoods in tight green terraces. I bite into one segment and think of summers spent pushing fruit segments onto earlobes pretending to be jade earrings. My cousin once cut her lobe accidentally with the rind; blood against green looked like avant-garde Christmas. She licked the wound, said it tasted like pennies and sunshine. Years later she emigrated to Calgary, expects snow next month. I send her a photo of pomelo peel sculpted into tiny lamps, candle flickering within. She texts back a selfie wearing mittens and grin so wide the latitude north of her smile serves as our only shared climate.

Last night I dream the balcony extends into ocean where still live manta rays their wings like obsidian cloak swallowing city reflections while their bellies glow. I am barefoot, tiles warm, water lapping. Neon from shop signs refracts into strings of hiragana which school me unwillingly into comprehension: なみ (wave), ひかり (light), かえる (to return). The rays circle slowly, half-ballet-half-ritual. Upon waking, I find that hiragana carves sound into my inner voice with precision previously unknown. Perhaps languages arrive unsolicited like dreams; we house them only if courage allows thin walls.

Milo announces closing the café’s physical premises next month. Rental hike eats fiction, spits out spreadsheets. We gather for a final brunch—imperfect crostinis topped with tempeh that tastes like resignation, also like hope marinated two days in tamarind. Customers write wishes on biodegradable spoons, plant them in planter boxes outside. I write: grow libraries between buildings. Soon after, a summer storm arrives obliterating ink, but seeds remain ingested by soil already smelling of turmeric and drainage. Months later, tiny sprouts emerge that no taxonomy recognises. Milo tells me on video call from Berlin where he now bartends, mixologist of endangered dialects. He says has not served a drink that does not taste faintly of monsoon. I tell him that counts tickets to nowhere are cheaper midnight Tuesdays. We promise nothing scheduled beyond breathing.

My father messages: vegetable prices quadrupled after another policy shift labelled reform. I ask if nostalgia tastes different in red soil. He forwards a photo of my childhood swing repurposed into support for guava branches heavy with fruit. Caption: productive nostalgia. Between us, 12-hour time difference becomes second spine. Time zones are just longitudes practising social distancing. We schedule awkward video dinners where he eats roti and I soup that steams across pixelated dateline. Once the internet lag makes his image freeze mid-laugh, moustache caught in shelter of partial sentence. I snapshot the frame, later draw it into zine portraying asynchronous father holding laughter like glitch between palms.

I learn to kintsugi my cracked ceramics with copper epoxy. Inside kitchen yellow light, the broken plate glints veins like rivers glowing on night satellite imagery. Imperfection elevated to cartography reminds me every map is memory stitched with intention. I stack dishes so they lean slightly, like towers narrated backwards. Apartment becomes museum of almosts: the kettle whose lid takes persuasion, a mirror flecked across which I drape fabric to soften myriad reflections, books tilting as though tides run through indices. One morning I catch myself straightening stack philosophical treatises not to align but to disturb angle again—discover desire for controlled chaos, distrust of perpendicular trust.  

Late October, the translator publishes her rendition, including footnote crediting nights spent decoding rust and river steam. Novel wins minor speck of award; next day newspaper reduces entire career to one paragraph. Sidelines observe how hyphenated identities broke sentence boundaries. Underneath another columnist laments literary metabolism accelerating beyond meaning. I screenshot for translator, she responds with a video taken from car window where roadsign shoots past too fast to parse. Over ambient wind she records herself saying: resistance is also a velocity. File ends before I reply. Speaking itself becomes stowaway narrative searching vessel porous as daylight.

National elections the following summer bring flyers wedged inside nooks nobody cleared since colonial drainage plans. On narrow lane outside tofu seller, young campaigners chant slogans about dignity while distributing packets of subsidised lentils branded with party colours. Neon confetti of promises sticks to boots in monsoon streets, swimming in gulley water until it resembles abstract expressionist canvases nobody would hang. I vote early in morning smelling faintly of rain residue on polyester queue banners. Thumb ink stains last three days, during which I binge documentaries about demolished trust. Each evening I watch the staticky footage with sound off, colours ebbing into the same grey as the ceiling stains that look like countries in miniature. I name them after emotions unpublishable: shameadonia, longtalia, forgivestan.

Sometime after, I wake with seedling from the planter box relocated to yoghurt cup on windowsill. Leaves curled like question marks typing weather into slow code. I overhead neighbour call it mung bean, but I acknowledge it as oracle. It grows against pane toward sunbeams that arrive slanted through security grating like piano bars. First tendril writes millimetered progress across dusty glass, a caption without sound. Every morning I greet it as one greets a dream deferred yet insistently leaning into fulfilment.  

I date seldom yet sometimes seek horizontal conversation. One liaison chooses rooftop of abandoned train car permanently tagged with spray paint whales. We drink tea from thermos, discuss how loneliness metabolises into texts composed at 3 a.m. but unsent until editing removes emotion like blight. She quotes Anne Carson about pain being a verb; I add that verbs conjugate but pain conjugates us. Later, we lie across rusted benches until dew collects on collar bones forming temporary tattoos of ignition. We do not exchange contact information, leave numbers like maybe in throat. Months later I pass train car painted over; where once whales flickered metal now announces an economic summit. I pause anyway, allow retina to superimpose what memory insists existed. Two lives intersect like chords resolving into relative minors neither chose at outset.

I give away white shirts one by one, retaining only the oldest that harbours slogan ghost. New ones arrive via thrift carrying smells of other wartorn laundries. Labels like desire you never consented to. I cut off collars, replace buttons with mismatched marble chips found in alley behind closed tile factory. Stitch by stitch event into wearable mosaic. Garment ends up looking like regret and celebration hooked at elbow, making out with mouths full of thread. First wearing it, violin upstairs changes piece to Vivaldi inside contrails. Coincidence claims no royalties; still, we tip in goosebumps.

Rumours swell that cinema lot might reopen not as nightclub but as venue for silent shadowplay where actors’ bodies silhouette behind screens of rice paper, audience seated among broken seats now upholstered by handwoven mats from displaced villages. Kickstarter campaign is crowd-surfed via stories smuggled through detention centre Wi-Fi. I pledge equivalent of one month instant-noodle allowance. Stretch goal promises skylight cut so real moon enters stagecraft. I imagine craters becoming uncredited cast members.

Winter here is absence rather than temperature. Jackets worn more like props. One Sunday I cycle outskirts where factories stand charred from arson several governments ago. Among twisted rebar floats bleached pallet marked fragile like paradox. I park, step inside roofless structure. Wind manipulates metal into whistles low enough to reset mammal heart rate. Within, graffiti overlays in languages I can’t alphabet, suddenly realised as coding instructions for unmanned drones. Morality feels brief. I leave but not before pocketing shard painted eye whose iris gradients from emerald to rust possibly 2007 colour palette. Upon homecoming I embed eye into soil of mung bean now sturdy enough to twine balcony railing like satellite interrogating unknown frequency. Weeks later yellow flowers embroiders green tapestry; pods hang shaped like eyelids closing. Bean is both pupil and prophet.

Gran once sewed quilts from fabric scraps including patch that read “secular prayers.” Square now travels countries at bottom of cousin’s recycled carry-on while she files taxes in Calgary winter. Stitching itself decomposes slowly via friction and sun, but phrase stays spelled. Gran left us earlier this unapologetic year when heart said I’ve beat enough for multiple civilisations. After, we found notebooks in Marathi within which she’d indexed all lullabies by decibel and provenance. Literal footnotes read: “sung slightly off-tempo to jolt baby awake, ensuring safe transition across railroad tracks dream.”  

I attend her remote memorial beneath banyan tree but no body—zodiac signs projected on leaves through hacked projector. Granduncle offers salted lassi to gathered collaborators unable to hug across bandwidth. Eulogies transmitted via bluetooth speakers that intermittently pick nearby Tamil pop radio. Sometimes grief notes highjack verses about longing, result accidental polyphony resembling hope misfiling grief under syncopation. By evening dispersed cousins create WhatsApp group titled “Inherited Itineraries,” where we drop pins corresponding leftovers of ancestral houses now restaurants, garment factories, parking garages. One pinned over Atlantic because ship manifest recorded great-grandfather harbouring lullaby kits sewn into jacket lining. We overlay his migration onto hurricane patterns, like proving breath and tempests share preferences. gran’s square labelled secular prayers becomes avatar icon; whenever someone uploads memory, image pixelates slightly as if fray decays into datastream. Thread archives only when last battery expires, iteration of digital samsara.

Letters cease arriving from translator. Rumour virus caught her lungs. Last message simply a screenshot of phone notes app containing line, “epilogue begins mid-sentence.” I print and fold small into matchbox labelled futures. Some weeks I carry it in pocket like talisman I refuse to strike. We build altars out of what refuses finality.  

City planners unveil floating wetland prototype made from recycled flip-flops and beheaded statues. Locals punt across lilypads to protest delays of pedestrian bridge linking university to heartware exchange programme training conflict mediators among street youth. Activists choreograph sit-ins choreographed as dancelike grey zones, raising fists while releasing helium carp balloons over construction site deemed river-stomach. Carp printed with QR codes linking to testimonials from disappeared academics. Scan underwater they become amphibious archives. Children chase balloons catching skyscrapers in their translucent bellies.  

I slip word “syncopated” into voicemail for friend now calendar-jumping through international fellowships; reply arrives as Insta-story poll: what texture is your current grief? Voters choose responses like silk bruised, gravel compressed, steel attempting violin. Result split even. She notes that audience left to their own devices mistake fractures for categories. I respond posting photo of ceramic plate now resembling topographic map of somewhere unnamed. Likes accumulate like frost. We agree that metaphor performs labour neither immigrant nor algorithm yet caught trafficking both.

Plantation drive NGO distributes saplings in tissue paper cones smelling eucalyptus. I adopt frangipani whose label warns slow establishment but eventual perfume capable dismantling territorial grief. It rides bus on my lap like newborn cramped between commuters sweating in office attire. Passengers glance then recollect no ordinance against plant attachment. Frangipani and I sit in aisle assigning backstories to strangers: woman with violin case travels incognito to violin inside wears bowtie as disguise. Whole commute becomes conspiracy of gentleness.  

On adoption day I wake with vertigo, entire floor at slight tilt as though city arthritic but hiding tremor. Doctor says benign positional but I recognise memory adjusting foundation. Later earthquake confirms diagnosis: plates imperceptibly shifting like awkward silences. People rush downstairs; I ascend carrying frangipani because falling plants feel horribly unfair. Rooftop sways like slow salsa. Carpenter neighbour already there hugs banister while smoking—claims this is his biennial retreat ritual. We watch neighbouring towers perform slow rock-a-bye, window glass leaping like glass whales breaching. Sirens below delineate injuries less structural than philosophical. When shaking subsides frangipani unpotted implies better view. We plant it inside coolant tank long disused but mineral water shaped hospitality. Cans dented like heavy punctuation, soil mixed with volcanic ash couriered from souvenir shop claiming eruption last century. Plant forgives us instantly. Thirty minutes later small white blooms open. Architect neighbour confesses made love on rooftop during childhood tremor when convinced apocalypse qualified as parental absence. We agree tremors are maybe parenthetical alertsens the earth inserts mid-story to without interrupting but reshaping syntax.  

Invitation to gallery opening reaches me via QR code graffiti I photograph while cycling over canal bridge. Exhibition titled Vacuum Sealed Love Notes Made of Bread. Artist vacuum-packs handwritten confessions inside loaves, which viewers slice open to reveal letter compressed into dough layers like geological scandal. I sneak bite despite stickers demanding, smell yeast of secrets fermented in hunger. Letter reads: “italicised within dough your future self remembers every sidewalk as tongue.” I chew and imagine horizon tasting like apology lightly salted. Note ends unsigned. That evening pocket crumbs into matchbox previously storing translator’s final sentence, making sandwich metaphor illegal in two countries.  

Parallel news drips: state budget cuts affect public libraries reduce opening hours to symbolic acknowledgment. Protesters arrange read-in outside closed doors, public literary naps recorded live for internet tourism. One librarian recites Jorge Luis Borges while balancing book on head about invisible labyrinth. Bystanders join until entire plaza becomes soft murmuring spine. Police arrive but cannot single out organizer because everyone holding book identical emergency print on newsprint labeled primer of ungovernability. City mayor concedes temporary reversal while comparing bibliophiles to viruses infinitely reproducible. Headline runs next day: “Virulent Literates Occupy Syntax.” Small win feels ceremonial like folding paper boat large enough to ferry marginalia across Styx.  

Autumn equinox night market celebrates equidistant sorrows by lighting 1000 paper lamps shaped obsolete currencies. Organizers insist symbolic combustion not fascist but collective sigh. I purchase one lamp printed former twenty-rupee note featuring salt march motif. Lamp seller advises write a debt, float lantern into canal so obligation dissolves mid-stream. I scribble: “for every silence rented due to insufficient vocabulary.” Flame takes like mischievous legislator. Lantern drifts past barges unloading pineapples, their tops like green flamenco fans mid-plié, impossibly celebratory.  

University announces residency for post-binary cartographers to remap borders without magnitudes. I apply proposing tactile maps molded from tea sediment and earthquake chalk. Acceptance email arrives sunset orange—colour until then merely descriptive. Residency begins January, twelve weeks iterating surfaces fingers can read without eyes. Fellow mappers include former origami master turned topographer of air currents, ex-banker decoding offshore scent signatures. One participant emerges from autistic schedule designing maps only visible under blacklight made from crustacean proteins—chart emerges like bruises blooming water. In first week we agree to include emotional elevation forgotten by GPS turn left cerebral roundabout. By third session prototype resembles sediment pages stacked resembling phyllo pastry but disabling singular destination instead layering arrivals alongside departures simultaneously like accordion breathing within frame. Matriarch of project, 67-year-old Javanese retired navigator, sings coordinates into wet plaster like ancestral chant. Sometimes lyrics mistranslate into submarine pulsars; we accept echolocation as narrative.  

Exhibition night, patrons feel islands they never traversed, brought to brink tears by demoted borders become gentle hill in palm. Government representative attending refuses to acknowledge that fingering grief qualifies as cartography, citing absence of angular borders key to nationalist health. Navigates mental breakdown by vomiting into field of collective sentiment, witnessed raw. Curator escorts official away but damage already reverberating across room; patrons decide to gift tactile map fragments as bandages for his mouth. We photograph tear-stained chunks guiding whispers back to touch ridges implying rebirth heading.

Mung bean now matures pods rattling like miniature castanets at bolero tempo. I harvest seeds redistributing among friends—some turned jewelry, others pressed into emergency currency, one mailed wrapped miniature quilt square labelled secular prayers. Existence expands via postage.  

Rooftop now colony: frangipani apparently bribed carpenter neighbour into building retaining wall from disco tiles rescued club demolition. Tiles arched around plant like ballroom skirts mid-curtsey. Next pot belatedly real carp pond, recycled from broken porcelain tubs fitted with gold-seamed cracks resembling our newly learned cartographies. sections of map waxed waterproof now withstand typhoon irrigation. Gold seams at first experimental now functional, apropos of which architect neighbour quotes Leonard Cohen: cracks where light also grows guavas. We harvest once; fruits too adolescent-tasting but laughter acceptable tree sap.

One dawn skywriter plane sketches word “perceive” above city in smoke letters dissipating by second syllable. Groundlings film vertical screens so eventually entire word absent aerial horizon only via thumb scroll. Author unknown, rumoured sponsored by foundation researching attention deficit historical loop. I bike in traffic watching smoke curl then vanish exactly like thought during zen koan except globally hyperlinked.  

Translator emerges aftermonths, not fully lung-functional but alive, seated wheelchair like throne carried by community bicycle brigade across library plaza where former read-ins now permanent pop-up microlibrary operating on goodwill barter. She hands me paper parcel sealed red twine. Inside single note: “found remainder of epilogue—reads simply ‘shh’ followed by ripple effect across languageless tide.” No period, only tide. I unfold the matchbox still carrying breadcrumb earth translator’s fragment; combine papers now in origami envelope folded from bus ticket bearing lime-coloured builder advertisement we both stood beneath that long ago. I float envelope into river via lotus boat; sun strikes translucency such that silver words momentarily become oar strokes. City witnesses through cracked morning commute windows but no one intervenes; they recognise a ceremony when they see it, maybe because everyone carries deserted yards inside fibre.

News arrives Nani’s quilts airlifted to gallery in archipelago ten thousand miles hence where curator exhibits alongside rice-sediment maps. Opening night features audience slowly unfolding quilts under moonlight until entire courtyard becomes constellation. Someone live-tweets thread quilt-names: ‘borderless, mothermouth, seaurethra.’ Trending hashtag lasts single tide cycle but archived by children somewhere repurposing memory into paper boats at drainage puddles.  

On final week of residency I ride train coastal utilising ticket mysteriously sulphur-coloured, conductor shrugs like spectra undisclosed. Windows frame seascapes resembling chalk pastel over oily glass. Salt crust gathers edges evoking maps no compass dared teach. I clutch urn tiny now containing remainder bean seeds labelled future map. Disembark onto estuary where lanterns once launched during equinox. Unknown children recognise seed symbolism, lead me to cove where maritime salvage artist welded old satellite dishes into coral reef supporting hydrophone playing whale breathe morse: “moisten borders.”  

Night brings full moon so close light triples subway tiles in ghost railway. Lampshade from abandoned lantern assembly line glows without electricity, powered reflection. Circle situ including mappers, musicians, caretakers forms spontaneous choir humming frequencies between human ear and tectonic. No microphones; only open sea. Translator wheelchaired to shoreline edge where sand meets waterline at diagonal impossible to bisect politically. She gestures silence swelling contained. We nod because understanding dawns coherent without grammar. Silence expands until swells tide pulsing against ankles inked with city’s distance-run. Holding seeds over water I release like offering. Pods disperse carried planetary circuitry—one drifts toward horizon, another spiral back beneath stilts supporting pier rotted almost musical. Seeds carried by current redesign shore; cart drawn morph unrendered by daylight.  

Reader, I could tally further coincidences, but balance sheet refuses obedience. Even narrative has travel visa. Better we exhale together observing city continue surface like skin atop immense restlessness. Every rooftop raises frangipani flags; quilt patches parachute intersections between pledge and actuality. Children learn equations equalise missing. Somewhere newly opened plaza fountain flows compass rose made coffee grounds; drink slowlypistol of compassion. Skywriter匿名 again next solstice, perhaps word “already” or maybe silence fills a predetermined acreage of blue.  

What remains unmanifest gathers breath within cyclical lungs like mung beans predicting earthquakes via root-shake semaphore. When asked direction, I reply toward the cracked, the gold-healed, the quietly bio-luminescent settling beneath calloused tongues. Home is temporary trespass between arrivals multiplying refusal to conform planar coordinates. Plate tectonics polite applause. Coda begins not with music but with steam rising rice pot, transparent syllables condensing porcelain lid into syllabary only grandmothers can almost read.  

From seven stories up, 6:17 a.m. kettle whistles at slightly off-key E-flat same as do

message.content:
The whistle of the 6:15 a.m. kettle always arrives before the sun does. In these thirty square metres a few floors above the tram line, morning is not just a time but a kind of weather that settles in the lungs. I open the balcony door to let it in—the smell of warm asphalt cooling after a humid night, wet newsprint steaming where the delivery cyclist folded it into a mailbox. Everything is marginally soft, like watercolour edges. For years I thought this softness was a weakness about the city itself, a collective giving-in to heat and humidity; I have since decided it is only patience. The city waits—not as we wait in queues, shifting anxiously from foot to foot, but as a cat waits, eyes half-closed, attuned to the slightest compact vibration of birdwing in dust-flecked sun.

There is, apparently, a limit to the number of times a human heart can break before it grows gilt along the fissures—shatter lines becoming seams of gold, the Japanese saying goes. I have counted mine carefully: once for every friendship that drifted under the colossal silence of careers and continents; once for every impossible attachment to someone who kept their life lightly compartmentalised, like a suitcase that could be zipped or unzipped in minutes. And yet every dawn the organ knocks at its cage, unruly, refusing even the dignity of ceramic irreparableness. I brew coffee in the single chipped press, watch the grounds bloom outwards like planets asserting gravitational dominance. A string of small galaxies inside a glass beaker. Maybe cosmology starts exactly here, in kitchenettes too cramped for anything except boiling water. First there is nothing, then there is a dark bloom expanding, then cream-coloured swirls form spiral arms around the void.

I leave the apartment earlier than necessary. A quarantine in the city left me irritably aware of stored air; I still push through doors as though expecting escape velocities. Down four flights sagging with monsoon moisture and the smoky afterthought of someone’s 2 a.m. curry, I emerge onto the footpath. The first blow of sunlight is full-contact; I recoil reflexively before walking straight into itsören-sweet smog. A builder, pale dust on his brow like mismatched foundation, nods because we recognise one another only in the way strangers recognise the recurring actors in each other’s daily mumblecore. I pass the corner cart that sells coconut-dipped fritters. The owner, Devi, hands the first one of each batch to an old stray the colour of thunderclouds who waits with noble patience—I once saw the man wear an entire plate on his head rather than let the dog go hungry. I nod at both of them. Neither bows. We are commoners in the same kingdom of briefly merciful shadows.  

At the river it is always seven degrees cooler, as though climate is negotiable when water is willing to mediate. I sit on the concrete bench where the algae creeps up the banks like inexperienced graffiti, green across pale grey. A boy sells jasmine bracelets from a wicker basket; I buy one, slide it over my wrist, and feel instantly ridiculous but almost ceremonially enrolled in the day. Sometimes, rarely, I write on napkins. Mostly I watch dragons curve between skyscrapers: construction cranes performing slow calligraphy against low cloud. My sister once said cities were invented for people who’d misunderstood smallness, whose nightmares expanded with too much rural sky. Maybe so. I feel safest when surrounded by millions who have no particular interest in me, just as benthic creatures drift unremarked in black water, bioluminescent only when they choose to be.

The café where I do dayshift proofreading has plywood salvaged from demolished churches for its interior tables—the grain of a hundred former confessional booths lives on inside gossip about rent hikes and casting calls. Milo, the owner, believes in fermentation the way others believe in portions of scripture: kombucha apostles in glass jars haloed by LED grow-lights; kimchi breathing quietly in the cool room like penitents fasting. We take our queues from orphaned cultures of bacteria claiming real estate. I like the symmetry. Late capitalism liquefies us and repurposes us into fizzy drink; meanwhile bacteria colonise our leftovers and produce something tangy and bright. Who’s exploiting whom, I ask you.

At 11:07 an email arrives from a translator in Quebec. She has come to an impasse with the author’s neologism “désertement,” a word coined for both abandonment and bar, simultaneously. French does this thing where verbs become nouns while staying verbs, refusing the fixed floor we erect between action and object. I type back that maybe English solved this elastic longing by inventing the present continuous, as if to concede the admission that nothing is ever quite done. I apologise for the extra syllables. The continuation of departure. Translation, I decide again, is not the ferrying of meaning across a river; it is the negotiation to build a third river where two once stood, complete with reeds and mosquitoes already indigenous to neither side.

By 12:30 the nausea of too much screen sets in. I walk to the art-supply store that smells like an impossible library of trees—cedar shavings, pine gum, ancient bark of extinct forests mashed into paper pulp. I buy nothing, only run my thumb along the sliced spiral sketchbooks whose cardboard backs still soak up the ambient guitar music leaking from upstairs. Somewhere above, a child learns chords that sound like minor-key lullabies written for borrowed sorrow. I remember learning to lie sideways across my twin bed at fourteen, ear to the wall, vibrating with every feet-sore roommate in next year’s unknown dormitory. Hope used to sound like distortion. Maybe still does.  

Across the street is the derelict cinema whose marquee dismantled itself into corrosion and pigeony vowels. Once, the carpets were midnight blue and the seats velvet like overripe plums. These days it hosts midnight pop-up raves where engineers project galaxies against the ruined ceiling and couples make out beneath imagined constellations. Entrance is donated canned goods; exit is remembering hunger can be both literal and figurative. I pass by the chained doors every week and still cannot decide whether what happens inside is vandalism or resurrection. Aren’t most of our living rooms just rebranded churches anyway—flatscreens for stained glass, binge serials for liturgies? We kneel on couches instead of prie-dieux. The popcorn tastes of salt and transubstantiation.

Time dilates when you live six stories up but refuse curtains. The sun becomes a chaperone, then an wallpaper. One year I papered one wall in maps, another in Polaroids taken before smartphones were gods. Different methods of making familiar territory. Somewhere I clipped the tail of a Metro ticket from Berlin dated 8.11.2010; the ink has cracked into tributaries. I remember stepping onto that platform with no luggage other than a battered Lonely Planet whose spine had surrendered at Prague, and we shared the car with three teenagers spraying cologne from miniature aerosol canisters, punctuating the new morning with scent like punctuation marks. First comma after home country; second semicolon before illegal bar. The boys sang poorly a pop song whose lyrics consisted mostly of heart repeated sixteen times, stretched until vowels sounded like skywriting. Somewhere between two stations they rearranged themselves into sleep. When I looked again, the cologne boy had curled against the window, breath fogging glass planted with tiny twin palm prints. Longing fossilised inside condensation.  

Back home in this equatorial tower block I note once more how the doorknob squeaks in E-flat, the exact key of the train lullaby. Sound memory carried halfway around the globe just to find a yellow kitchen door. I stand there, keys in hand, still tasting Berlin’s air blown across eight time zones like balloon mail. Sometimes geography is merely a insistence on resonance. I do not cry about it; the body already hoards humidity like coin. Instead I put water to boil for pasta, add salt until the pot tastes like beach break at low tide. An exhaust fan sighs like a mother who’s finally seated after days.  

At night the wet markets persuade the street into neon haze. Banners hung at diagonals promising 3-D pork and lottery T-shirts. You can buy twenty-five kinds of rice from unmilled ruby grains the colour of hacked pomegranate seeds, to translucent basmati that smells faintly of pandan. Stallholders perform card tricks with plastic bags and receipts. I buy a stalk of lotus root to carve into stamps. There is no practical reason; merely the desire to press a cross-sectioned canal network into saffron ink and see blossoms appear on scrap paper, like cities with radial arteries nobody asked for but now cannot live without.

I own four identical white shirts because decision fatigue accumulates interest; also because the blankness invites projection. When I cycle across the bridge at dusk, headlamp flickering over rivets the colour of dried blood, I imagine the shirts are pages. At red lights, exhaust slides across the cotton until it carries glyphs in grime. I arrive home smelling of motor spirit and carbonised sugar: evidence I have been read, annotated, margin-scribbled by the city’s hurrying hand.  

One shirt still bears the faint bleached mark of a campaign slogan from 2017. I marched that March with two friends who since moved away. We linked arms under banyans older than most constellations. Later the same night at a 24-hour dim sum diner, we invented a religion based on the steam rising from baskets of har gow—the translucent crescents a sacrament, shrimp bright orange prayer beads. Practitioners held silence for exactly four minutes between replenishment cycles. It felt hilariously essential until the police outside dispersed another protest somewhere else; then the laughter tasted like cardboard. Still, somebody tore a hole in the rhetoric of inevitability, stitch by stitch, even if only by demanding bathrooms for trans elders in that fluorescent corridor. The mark faded to peroxide ghost. Mostly I forget, but when I fold laundry the phrase threads itself across fabric like latent Braille.  

Memory sometimes stacks like mise-en-abyme: I remember being six on my grandmother’s veranda. In those years, monsoons were still heroic events thundering across the bay like Wagnerian cavalry. I stood barefoot on cool tiles, watching water lash coconut palms. Nani told me storms were the sky giving itself a haircut; that day it chose mohawk. Electricity went out for forty-eight hours. We ate dinner by lamp flame, shadows capering across vaulted ceilings painted sky-blue decades earlier, now gone tobacco with leaks. Between us and the lantern flame hovered moths fat as hail. Nani recited couplets in a dialect that no longer has a script; I mimic-knotted them around my tongue decades later when speaking to friends, not knowing I carried bruised phonemes in my mouth still colouring every accent I acquired. That night, after rain slackened into caesura, she poured kerosene over termite-ridden beams to keep them from collapsing. The smell combined petrichor and flammable warning. Scent memory so precise I bottled it for years inside a note that simply said summer smells like survival.  

Back to present tense dusk. The shirt is hung up. I cook spinach with garlic that snaps like cheap fireworks. My phone buzzes: group chat debating whether nostalgia is a bourgeois indulgence. One friend currently teaching refugee kids from a makeshift classroom in a disused shopping mall responds with a photo of children re-enacting tag using shadows from a single floodlight. No colour except silver skin and darkness. Caption: “We talk of nostalgia because we still believe tomorrow is a place.” The thread goes quiet until a thumbs-up emoji ambles in like an apology. I set tomatoes to blister on the pan. I do not caption the steam of my kitchen; it is enough I am witness.

At 10 p.m. the upstairs neighbour practices violin again, same Bach prelude turned minor by relentless humidity. At university she studied astrophysics; music is now her rebellion against measurable distances. Sometimes she sways forward and the bow<|reserved_token_163766|>es against climate, then retreats. Through the plaster ceiling I imagine her brain dense as a star catalogue. Sound leaks like constellations half occluded. I write longhand letters I never send because email destroys correlation between scrawl and pulse. By midnight the wrist cramps alphabet railcars running off track, but I persist until ink resembles blood on wood pulp. I address them to every self Iitively past stranger I failed to thank, including the cologne boys in Berlin, the museum guard who looked away when I cried in front of Käthe Kollwitz’s mother embracing dead son, the cat in Kyoto who led me through alleys one evening to avoid a salaryman who had followed since Hakani-cho tram stop. I apologise in swerves of pen for the brevity of kindness and promise I still practice swimming through grief’s narrow lane. I fold pages into lotus boats, float them in a salad bowl of tap water. They disintegrate slowly, fibres drifting like planktonic paper lanterns en route to some fictional horizon.  

Three times a year I take night bus to the coast where bio-luminescent plankton mimic chopped galaxies in the shallows. Amateurs bring buckets expecting fireworks; locals understand the light is shy. You have to apologize for existing in sneakers, stand ankle-deep and wait for pupils to dilate. Then the sand appears star-flecked, and if you walk each footfall bursts turquoise, an alphabet spelling nothing every step. Once a man next to me wept—not dramatic, just tear-tracks gleaming—then confided softly his daughter loved stars but died before second birthday. We never exchanged names; the bioluminescence profiled his grief like silver. At dawn he’d disappeared, but the beach remembered his outline, a depression shaped like the constellation Aquila. I still send spoken postcards into that symmetry whenever sea breeze reaches through my bedroom window via turbine engines of distant freighters.

Online spaces teach me that we are snow globes continuously falling on ourselves. Algorithms plastic-wrap the past into scrollable highlights, severing cause from consequence so that every outrage feels immediate but never cumulative. One morning a thread about gender-neutral housing policies derails into someone claiming architecture is inherently misogynistic because doors impose masculine vertical hierarchies. Within three minutes an elder chimes in with a photo of ceremonial Tajik pillars carved open like lotus ribs to permit even moonlight horizontal passage. Beneath it a comment in Comic Sans simply says “windows are thresholds in drag.” 6k likes. The absurdity does not satiate; it merely displaces. I close the tab and stare out at a construction pit across the street. Men in neon vests are pouring concrete. One wipes his forehead with a flag the exact lime of tropical algae. The metaphor leaks: what bruises beneath ideology is still the wanting muscle. Wipe. Pour. Set.

Some nights I wake to the refrigerator humming in key with the violin upstairs and think the city is harmonising itself insomnia by insomnia. Between 3 and 4 a.m., adrenaline thins into something spiritual but not yet merciful. I sit on the balcony dangling bare feet above traffic long gone. Neon signs ghost on shuttered façades, constellations of commerce blinking still. A solitary pedestrian passes below in headphones, moving arms subtly as though conducting an unseen orchestra. I imagine him hearing the same syncopation I hear: drone of air-con, pulse in throat, the slight buzz from streetlight capacitor. For moments the script switches and the city scores us. Maybe compositions finish only by abandoning bar lines; maybe living is generous improvisation obligating no coda.

The calendar forgives nobody. One July the monsoon fails and water rations begin. We queue with mismatched containers at tankers manned by men who look like sons of former farmers. Their eyes carry dunes. Elderly women hand around peanut candy because the shiver of blood sugar feels like solidarity convertible to moisture. Children play hopscotch beside oil drums labelled potable. A woman in batik sarong recites headlines aloud like misquoted lullabies about glaciers whose elderly memory hovers above their former mass like steam above cooling tectonics. We nod, balancing jugs on hips. My neighbour, an architect, tells me every building blueprint contains a ghost of the river it displaced; we are drinking ancestral runoff todayly disguised. That day the government flies in bottled water branded as charity by conglomerates who lobbied the drought. We take it gratefully, shamefully. Desperation outsources ethics along with manufacturing. I stack the 1.5-litre bottles against my kitchen wall like prayer candles in reverse: we light them by uncapping mouths.  

Two metres away in a different universe, a millionaire live-streams his eco-villa reclaimed from plastic waste. Hashtag #OceanToHome appears on billboards while hand-pumps in peri-urban districts cough rust. I scroll past sunset filtering through an advertisement for carbon offsets, fluorescent orange like sunburn on algorithm. Disconnect. I press the blistered tomatoes into sauce, feed myself with what privilege reduces to mundane verbs: chop, simmer, salt, breathe.

Letters keep coming from the translator, who has now invented the neologism into “unmooreding,” a gerund staying forever in motion yet grammatically homeless. I reply with a photograph of the salad bowl harbouring disintegrated confessions—fibres still swirl like plankton. I type: “I think pdfs can mourn, too.” She answers with the laughing-crying emoji and a video of her son hammering piano keys with both fists, composition entitled “The House That Ran Away.” None of us runs; we merely surrender different organs.  

Autumn here does not arrive with gold leaf, but with a gradation of mildew. One day the air becomes precise, smelling of pencil shavings and distance. I take jacket from suitcase that still smells of cedar mothballs my mother packed in the year I first left for college. It resolutely refuses to fit, but I wear it hanging open like a negotiator with the breeze. The river drifts slower, as though processing metaphors. Dragon boat racers stow paddles; empty buoys gleam like orthodontic overbites. In market, vendors begin arranging pomelos the size of childhoods in tight green terraces. I bite into one segment and think of summers spent pushing fruit segments onto earlobes pretending to be jade earrings. My cousin once cut her lobe accidentally with the rind; blood against green looked like avant-garde Christmas. She licked the wound, said it tasted like pennies and sunshine. Years later she emigrated to Calgary, expects snow next month. I send her a photo of pomelo peel sculpted into tiny lamps, candle flickering within. She texts back a selfie wearing mittens and grin so wide the latitude north of her smile serves as our only shared climate.

Last night I dream the balcony extends into ocean where still live manta rays their wings like obsidian cloak swallowing city reflections while their bellies glow. I am barefoot, tiles warm, water lapping. Neon from shop signs refracts into strings of hiragana which school me unwillingly into comprehension: なみ (wave), ひかり (light), かえる (to return). The rays circle slowly, half-ballet-half-ritual. Upon waking, I find that hiragana carves sound into my inner voice with precision previously unknown. Perhaps languages arrive unsolicited like dreams; we house them only if courage allows thin walls.

Milo announces closing the café’s physical premises next month. Rental hike eats fiction, spits out spreadsheets. We gather for a final brunch—imperfect crostinis topped with tempeh that tastes like resignation, also like hope marinated two days in tamarind. Customers write wishes on biodegradable spoons, plant them in planter boxes outside. I write: grow libraries between buildings. Soon after, a summer storm arrives obliterating ink, but seeds remain ingested by soil already smelling of turmeric and drainage. Months later, tiny sprouts emerge that no taxonomy recognises. Milo tells me on video call from Berlin where he now bartends, mixologist of endangered dialects. He says has not served a drink that does not taste faintly of monsoon. I tell him that counts tickets to nowhere are cheaper midnight Tuesdays. We promise nothing scheduled beyond breathing.

My father messages: vegetable prices quadrupled after another policy shift labelled reform. I ask if nostalgia tastes different in red soil. He forwards a photo of my childhood swing repurposed into support for guava branches heavy with fruit. Caption: productive nostalgia. Between us, 12-hour time difference becomes second spine. Time zones are just longitudes practising social distancing. We schedule awkward video dinners where he eats roti and I soup that steams across pixelated dateline. Once the internet lag makes his image freeze mid-laugh, moustache caught in shelter of partial sentence. I snapshot the frame, later draw it into zine portraying asynchronous father holding laughter like glitch between palms.

I learn to kintsugi my cracked ceramics with copper epoxy. Inside kitchen yellow light, the broken plate glints veins like rivers glowing on night satellite imagery. Imperfection elevated to cartography reminds me every map is memory stitched with intention. I stack dishes so they lean slightly, like towers narrated backwards. Apartment becomes museum of almosts: the kettle whose lid takes persuasion, a mirror flecked across which I drape fabric to soften myriad reflections, books tilting as though tides run through indices. One morning I catch myself straightening stack philosophical treatises not to align but to disturb angle again—discover desire for controlled chaos, distrust of perpendicular trust.  

Late October, the translator publishes her rendition, including footnote crediting nights spent decoding rust and river steam. Novel wins minor speck of award; next day newspaper reduces entire career to one paragraph. Sidelines observe how hyphenated identities broke sentence boundaries. Underneath another columnist laments literary metabolism accelerating beyond meaning. I screenshot for translator, she responds with a video taken from car window where roadsign shoots past too fast to parse. Over ambient wind she records herself saying: resistance is also a velocity. File ends before I reply. Speaking itself becomes stowaway narrative searching vessel porous as daylight.

National elections the following summer bring flyers wedged inside nooks nobody cleared since colonial drainage plans. On narrow lane outside tofu seller, young campaigners chant slogans about dignity while distributing packets of subsidised lentils branded with party colours. Neon confetti of promises sticks to boots in monsoon streets, swimming in gulley water until it resembles abstract expressionist canvases nobody would hang. I vote early in morning smelling faintly of rain residue on polyester queue banners. Thumb ink stains last three days, during which I binge documentaries about demolished trust. Each evening I watch the staticky footage with sound off, colours ebbing into the same grey as the ceiling stains that look like countries in miniature. I name them after emotions unpublishable: shameadonia, longtalia, forgivestan.

Sometime after, I wake with seedling from the planter box relocated to yoghurt cup on windowsill. Leaves curled like question marks typing weather into slow code. I overhead neighbour call it mung bean, but I acknowledge it as oracle. It grows against pane toward sunbeams that arrive slanted through security grating like piano bars. First tendril writes millimetered progress across dusty glass, a caption without sound. Every morning I greet it as one greets a dream deferred yet insistently leaning into fulfilment.  

I date seldom yet sometimes seek horizontal conversation. One liaison chooses rooftop of abandoned train car permanently tagged with spray paint whales. We drink tea from thermos, discuss how loneliness metabolises into texts composed at 3 a.m. but unsent until editing removes emotion like blight. She quotes Anne Carson about pain being a verb; I add that verbs conjugate but pain conjugates us. Later, we lie across rusted benches until dew collects on collar bones forming temporary tattoos of ignition. We do not exchange contact information, leave numbers like maybe in throat. Months later I pass train car painted over; where once whales flickered metal now announces an economic summit. I pause anyway, allow retina to superimpose what memory insists existed. Two lives intersect like chords resolving into relative minors neither chose at outset.

I give away white shirts one by one, retaining only the oldest that harbours slogan ghost. New ones arrive via thrift carrying smells of other wartorn laundries. Labels like desire you never consented to. I cut off collars, replace buttons with mismatched marble chips found in alley behind closed tile factory. Stitch by stitch event into wearable mosaic. Garment ends up looking like regret and celebration hooked at elbow, making out with mouths full of thread. First wearing it, violin upstairs changes piece to Vivaldi inside contrails. Coincidence claims no royalties; still, we tip in goosebumps.

Rumours swell that cinema lot might reopen not as nightclub but as venue for silent shadowplay where actors’ bodies silhouette behind screens of rice paper, audience seated among broken seats now upholstered by handwoven mats from displaced villages. Kickstarter campaign is crowd-surfed via stories smuggled through detention centre Wi-Fi. I pledge equivalent of one month instant-noodle allowance. Stretch goal promises skylight cut so real moon enters stagecraft. I imagine craters becoming uncredited cast members.

Winter here is absence rather than temperature. Jackets worn more like props. One Sunday I cycle outskirts where factories stand charred from arson several governments ago. Among twisted rebar floats bleached pallet marked fragile like paradox. I park, step inside roofless structure. Wind manipulates metal into whistles low enough to reset mammal heart rate. Within, graffiti overlays in languages I can’t alphabet, suddenly realised as coding instructions for unmanned drones. Morality feels brief. I leave but not before pocketing shard painted eye whose iris gradients from emerald to rust possibly 2007 colour palette. Upon homecoming I embed eye into soil of mung bean now sturdy enough to twine balcony railing like satellite interrogating unknown frequency. Weeks later yellow flowers embroiders green tapestry; pods hang shaped like eyelids closing. Bean is both pupil and prophet.

Gran once sewed quilts from fabric scraps including patch that read “secular prayers.” Square now travels countries at bottom of cousin’s recycled carry-on while she files taxes in Calgary winter. Stitching itself decomposes slowly via friction and sun, but phrase stays spelled. Gran left us earlier this unapologetic year when heart said I’ve beat enough for multiple civilisations. After, we found notebooks in Marathi within which she’d indexed all lullabies by decibel and provenance. Literal footnotes read: “sung slightly off-tempo to jolt baby awake, ensuring safe transition across railroad tracks dream.”  

I attend her remote memorial beneath banyan tree but no body—zodiac signs projected on leaves through hacked projector. Granduncle offers salted lassi to gathered collaborators unable to hug across bandwidth. Eulogies transmitted via bluetooth speakers that intermittently pick nearby Tamil pop radio. Sometimes grief notes highjack verses about longing, result accidental polyphony resembling hope misfiling grief under syncopation. By evening dispersed cousins create WhatsApp group titled “Inherited Itineraries,” where we drop pins corresponding leftovers of ancestral houses now restaurants, garment factories, parking garages. One pinned over Atlantic because ship manifest recorded great-grandfather harbouring lullaby kits sewn into jacket lining. We overlay his migration onto hurricane patterns, like proving breath and tempests share preferences. gran’s square labelled secular prayers becomes avatar icon; whenever someone uploads memory, image pixelates slightly as if fray decays into datastream. Thread archives only when last battery expires, iteration of digital samsara.

Letters cease arriving from translator. Rumour virus caught her lungs. Last message simply a screenshot of phone notes app containing line, “epilogue begins mid-sentence.” I print and fold small into matchbox labelled futures. Some weeks I carry it in pocket like talisman I refuse to strike. We build altars out of what refuses finality.  

City planners unveil floating wetland prototype made from recycled flip-flops and beheaded statues. Locals punt across lilypads to protest delays of pedestrian bridge linking university to heartware exchange programme training conflict mediators among street youth. Activists choreograph sit-ins choreographed as dancelike grey zones, raising fists while releasing helium carp balloons over construction site deemed river-stomach. Carp printed with QR codes linking to testimonials from disappeared academics. Scan underwater they become amphibious archives. Children chase balloons catching skyscrapers in their translucent bellies.  

I slip word “syncopated” into voicemail for friend now calendar-jumping through international fellowships; reply arrives as Insta-story poll: what texture is your current grief? Voters choose responses like silk bruised, gravel compressed, steel attempting violin. Result split even. She notes that audience left to their own devices mistake fractures for categories. I respond posting photo of ceramic plate now resembling topographic map of somewhere unnamed. Likes accumulate like frost. We agree that metaphor performs labour neither immigrant nor algorithm yet caught trafficking both.

Plantation drive NGO distributes saplings in tissue paper cones smelling eucalyptus. I adopt frangipani whose label warns slow establishment but eventual perfume capable dismantling territorial grief. It rides bus on my lap like newborn cramped between commuters sweating in office attire. Passengers glance then recollect no ordinance against plant attachment. Frangipani and I sit in aisle assigning backstories to strangers: woman with violin case travels incognito to violin inside wears bowtie as disguise. Whole commute becomes conspiracy of gentleness.  

On adoption day I wake with vertigo, entire floor at slight tilt as though city arthritic but hiding tremor. Doctor says benign positional but I recognise memory adjusting foundation. Later earthquake confirms diagnosis: plates imperceptibly shifting like awkward silences. People rush downstairs; I ascend carrying frangipani because falling plants feel horribly unfair. Rooftop sways like slow salsa. Carpenter neighbour already there hugs banister while smoking—claims this is his biennial retreat ritual. We watch neighbouring towers perform slow rock-a-bye, window glass leaping like glass whales breaching. Sirens below delineate injuries less structural than philosophical. When shaking subsides frangipani unpotted implies better view. We plant it inside coolant tank long disused but mineral water shaped hospitality. Cans dented like heavy punctuation, soil mixed with volcanic ash couriered from souvenir shop claiming eruption last century. Plant forgives us instantly. Thirty minutes later small white blooms open. Architect neighbour confesses made love on rooftop during childhood tremor when convinced apocalypse qualified as parental absence. We agree tremors are maybe parenthetical alertsens the earth inserts mid-story to without interrupting but reshaping syntax.  

Invitation to gallery opening reaches me via QR code graffiti I photograph while cycling over canal bridge. Exhibition titled Vacuum Sealed Love Notes Made of Bread. Artist vacuum-packs handwritten confessions inside loaves, which viewers slice open to reveal letter compressed into dough layers like geological scandal. I sneak bite despite stickers demanding, smell yeast of secrets fermented in hunger. Letter reads: “italicised within dough your future self remembers every sidewalk as tongue.” I chew and imagine horizon tasting like apology lightly salted. Note ends unsigned. That evening pocket crumbs into matchbox previously storing translator’s final sentence, making sandwich metaphor illegal in two countries.  

Parallel news drips: state budget cuts affect public libraries reduce opening hours to symbolic acknowledgment. Protesters arrange read-in outside closed doors, public literary naps recorded live for internet tourism. One librarian recites Jorge Luis Borges while balancing book on head about invisible labyrinth. Bystanders join until entire plaza becomes soft murmuring spine. Police arrive but cannot single out organizer because everyone holding book identical emergency print on newsprint labeled primer of ungovernability. City mayor concedes temporary reversal while comparing bibliophiles to viruses infinitely reproducible. Headline runs next day: “Virulent Literates Occupy Syntax.” Small win feels ceremonial like folding paper boat large enough to ferry marginalia across Styx.  

Autumn equinox night market celebrates equidistant sorrows by lighting 1000 paper lamps shaped obsolete currencies. Organizers insist symbolic combustion not fascist but collective sigh. I purchase one lamp printed former twenty-rupee note featuring salt march motif. Lamp seller advises write a debt, float lantern into canal so obligation dissolves mid-stream. I scribble: “for every silence rented due to insufficient vocabulary.” Flame takes like mischievous legislator. Lantern drifts past barges unloading pineapples, their tops like green flamenco fans mid-plié, impossibly celebratory.  

University announces residency for post-binary cartographers to remap borders without magnitudes. I apply proposing tactile maps molded from tea sediment and earthquake chalk. Acceptance email arrives sunset orange—colour until then merely descriptive. Residency begins January, twelve weeks iterating surfaces fingers can read without eyes. Fellow mappers include former origami master turned topographer of air currents, ex-banker decoding offshore scent signatures. One participant emerges from autistic schedule designing maps only visible under blacklight made from crustacean proteins—chart emerges like bruises blooming water. In first week we agree to include emotional elevation forgotten by GPS turn left cerebral roundabout. By third session prototype resembles sediment pages stacked resembling phyllo pastry but disabling singular destination instead layering arrivals alongside departures simultaneously like accordion breathing within frame. Matriarch of project, 67-year-old Javanese retired navigator, sings coordinates into wet plaster like ancestral chant. Sometimes lyrics mistranslate into submarine pulsars; we accept echolocation as narrative.  

Exhibition night, patrons feel islands they never traversed, brought to brink tears by demoted borders become gentle hill in palm. Government representative attending refuses to acknowledge that fingering grief qualifies as cartography, citing absence of angular borders key to nationalist health. Navigates mental breakdown by vomiting into field of collective sentiment, witnessed raw. Curator escorts official away but damage already reverberating across room; patrons decide to gift tactile map fragments as bandages for his mouth. We photograph tear-stained chunks guiding whispers back to touch ridges implying rebirth heading.

Mung bean now matures pods rattling like miniature castanets at bolero tempo. I harvest seeds redistributing among friends—some turned jewelry, others pressed into emergency currency, one mailed wrapped miniature quilt square labelled secular prayers. Existence expands via postage.  

Rooftop now colony: frangipani apparently bribed carpenter neighbour into building retaining wall from disco tiles rescued club demolition. Tiles arched around plant like ballroom skirts mid-curtsey. Next pot belatedly real carp pond, recycled from broken porcelain tubs fitted with gold-seamed cracks resembling our newly learned cartographies. sections of map waxed waterproof now withstand typhoon irrigation. Gold seams at first experimental now functional, apropos of which architect neighbour quotes Leonard Cohen: cracks where light also grows guavas. We harvest once; fruits too adolescent-tasting but laughter acceptable tree sap.

One dawn skywriter plane sketches word “perceive” above city in smoke letters dissipating by second syllable. Groundlings film vertical screens so eventually entire word absent aerial horizon only via thumb scroll. Author unknown, rumoured sponsored by foundation researching attention deficit historical loop. I bike in traffic watching smoke curl then vanish exactly like thought during zen koan except globally hyperlinked.  

Translator emerges aftermonths, not fully lung-functional but alive, seated wheelchair like throne carried by community bicycle brigade across library plaza where former read-ins now permanent pop-up microlibrary operating on goodwill barter. She hands me paper parcel sealed red twine. Inside single note: “found remainder of epilogue—reads simply ‘shh’ followed by ripple effect across languageless tide.” No period, only tide. I unfold the matchbox still carrying breadcrumb earth translator’s fragment; combine papers now in origami envelope folded from bus ticket bearing lime-coloured builder advertisement we both stood beneath that long ago. I float envelope into river via lotus boat; sun strikes translucency such that silver words momentarily become oar strokes. City witnesses through cracked morning commute windows but no one intervenes; they recognise a ceremony when they see it, maybe because everyone carries deserted yards inside fibre.

News arrives Nani’s quilts airlifted to gallery in archipelago ten thousand miles hence where curator exhibits alongside rice-sediment maps. Opening night features audience slowly unfolding quilts under moonlight until entire courtyard becomes constellation. Someone live-tweets thread quilt-names: ‘borderless, mothermouth, seaurethra.’ Trending hashtag lasts single tide cycle but archived by children somewhere repurposing memory into paper boats at drainage puddles.  

On final week of residency I ride train coastal utilising ticket mysteriously sulphur-coloured, conductor shrugs like spectra undisclosed. Windows frame seascapes resembling chalk pastel over oily glass. Salt crust gathers edges evoking maps no compass dared teach. I clutch urn tiny now containing remainder bean seeds labelled future map. Disembark onto estuary where lanterns once launched during equinox. Unknown children recognise seed symbolism, lead me to cove where maritime salvage artist welded old satellite dishes into coral reef supporting hydrophone playing whale breathe morse: “moisten borders.”  

Night brings full moon so close light triples subway tiles in ghost railway. Lampshade from abandoned lantern assembly line glows without electricity, powered reflection. Circle situ including mappers, musicians, caretakers forms spontaneous choir humming frequencies between human ear and tectonic. No microphones; only open sea. Translator wheelchaired to shoreline edge where sand meets waterline at diagonal impossible to bisect politically. She gestures silence swelling contained. We nod because understanding dawns coherent without grammar. Silence expands until swells tide pulsing against ankles inked with city’s distance-run. Holding seeds over water I release like offering. Pods disperse carried planetary circuitry—one drifts toward horizon, another spiral back beneath stilts supporting pier rotted almost musical. Seeds carried by current redesign shore; cart drawn morph unrendered by daylight.  

Reader, I could tally further coincidences, but balance sheet refuses obedience. Even narrative has travel visa. Better we exhale together observing city continue surface like skin atop immense restlessness. Every rooftop raises frangipani flags; quilt patches parachute intersections between pledge and actuality. Children learn equations equalise missing. Somewhere newly opened plaza fountain flows compass rose made coffee grounds; drink slowlypistol of compassion. Skywriter匿名 again next solstice, perhaps word “already” or maybe silence fills a predetermined acreage of blue.  

What remains unmanifest gathers breath within cyclical lungs like mung beans predicting earthquakes via root-shake semaphore. When asked direction, I reply toward the cracked, the gold-healed, the quietly bio-luminescent settling beneath calloused tongues. Home is temporary trespass between arrivals multiplying refusal to conform planar coordinates. Plate tectonics polite applause. Coda begins not with music but with steam rising rice pot, transparent syllables condensing porcelain lid into syllabary only grandmothers can almost read.  

From seven stories up, 6:17 a.m. kettle whistles at slightly off-key E-flat same as do

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
